arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records * regardless of compression. * *

Thread-safety: RecordBatch implementations are not thread-safe. * Batches backed by ByteBuffer are particularly vulnerable to concurrent modification. Multiple threads * should not access the same batch concurrently. However, different batches can be safely accessed by * different threads in parallel. * *

Usage: Record batches are typically accessed through {@link MemoryRecords} or * {@link Records} implementations, which manage the underlying buffer lifecycle. Direct instantiation * of batch implementations is uncommon in normal usage. */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable { /** * The "magic" values */ byte MAGIC_VALUE_V0 = 0; byte MAGIC_VALUE_V1 = 1; byte MAGIC_VALUE_V2 = 2; /** * The current "magic" value */ byte CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE = MAGIC_VALUE_V2; /** * Timestamp value for records without a timestamp */ long NO_TIMESTAMP = -1L; /** * Values used in the v2 record format by non-idempotent/non-transactional producers or when * up-converting from an older format. */ long NO_PRODUCER_ID = -1L; short NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH = -1; int NO_SEQUENCE = -1; /** * Used to indicate an unknown leader epoch, which will be the case when the record set is * first created by the producer. */ int NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH = -1; /** * Check whether the checksum of this batch is correct. * * @return true if the checksum is valid, false otherwise */ boolean isValid(); /** * Raise an exception if the checksum is not valid. * * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.CorruptRecordException if the checksum is invalid */ void ensureValid(); /** * Get the checksum of this record batch, which covers the batch header as well as all of the records. * Uses CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial. * * @return The 4-byte unsigned checksum represented as a long */ long checksum(); /** * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch. * * If the timestamp type is create time ({@link TimestampType#CREATE_TIME}), this is the max * timestamp among all records contained in this batch and the value is updated during log compaction. * If the timestamp type is log append time, this value is set by the broker. * * @return The max timestamp in milliseconds */ long maxTimestamp(); /** * Get the timestamp type of this record batch. This will be {@link TimestampType#NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE} * if the batch has magic 0. * * @return The timestamp type, never null */ TimestampType timestampType(); /** * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the * behavior is equivalent. * *

Performance note: For older magic versions (0 and 1), this method may require * iterating through records to determine the base offset. Use {@link #lastOffset()} instead if efficiency * is critical, as it is always efficient across all magic versions. * * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record * as described above). */ long baseOffset(); /** * Get the last offset in this record batch (inclusive). Just like {@link #baseOffset()}, the last offset * always reflects the offset of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log * compaction. * *

Performance note: This method is efficient for all magic versions and does not * require record iteration. * * @return The offset of the last record in this batch */ long lastOffset(); /** * Get the offset following this record batch (i.e. the last offset contained in this batch plus one). * * @return the next consecutive offset following this batch */ long nextOffset(); /** * Get the record format version of this record batch (i.e its magic value). * * @return the magic byte (0, 1, or 2) */ byte magic(); /** * Get the producer id for this log record batch. For older magic versions, this will return * {@link #NO_PRODUCER_ID} (-1). * *

The producer id is used for idempotence and transactional semantics. Only magic version 2+ * batches can have meaningful producer IDs. * * @return The producer id or {@link #NO_PRODUCER_ID} if there is none */ long producerId(); /** * Get the producer epoch for this log record batch. For older magic versions, this will return * {@link #NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} (-1). * *

The producer epoch is paired with the producer ID to support idempotence. Epochs are incremented * when a producer is fenced by a new generation. * * @return The producer epoch, or {@link #NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} if there is none */ short producerEpoch(); /** * Does the batch have a valid producer id set. A batch has a valid producer id if the id is greater than * {@link #NO_PRODUCER_ID}. * * @return true if this batch has a valid producer id, false otherwise */ boolean hasProducerId(); /** * Get the base sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #baseOffset()}, this value is not * affected by compaction: it always retains the base sequence number from the original batch. * *

The sequence number is used for idempotent and transactional producers to detect duplicates and * out-of-order messages. * * @return The first sequence number or {@link #NO_SEQUENCE} if there is none */ int baseSequence(); /** * Get the last sequence number of this record batch. Like {@link #lastOffset()}, the last sequence number * always reflects the sequence number of the last record in the original batch, even if it is removed during log * compaction. * * @return The last sequence number or {@link #NO_SEQUENCE} if there is none */ int lastSequence(); /** * Get the compression type of this record batch. * * @return The compression type ({@link CompressionType#NONE}, {@link CompressionType#GZIP}, * {@link CompressionType#SNAPPY}, {@link CompressionType#LZ4}, or {@link CompressionType#ZSTD}) */ CompressionType compressionType(); /** * Get the size in bytes of this batch, including the size of all records and the batch overhead (header). * *

Performance note: This is an O(1) operation. * * @return The size in bytes of this batch */ int sizeInBytes(); /** * Get the count of records in this batch if it is efficiently supported by the record format. * This is only efficiently supported for magic version 2 and higher. * *

Performance note: For magic 0 and 1, returns null to indicate that record * count is not readily available without iteration. * * @return The number of records in the batch, or null for magic versions 0 and 1. */ Integer countOrNull(); /** * Check whether this record batch is compressed. * * @return true if the batch uses any compression codec, false if uncompressed ({@link CompressionType#NONE}) */ boolean isCompressed(); /** * Write this record batch into a buffer. The batch is serialized in its entirety, including headers * and all records. * * @param buffer The buffer to write the batch to. The buffer must have sufficient remaining space * for the entire batch ({@link #sizeInBytes()}) * @throws java.nio.BufferOverflowException if the buffer does not have sufficient remaining space */ void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer); /** * Whether or not this record batch is part of a transaction. * *

Transactional batches contain records from a single atomic operation and will either all be committed * or all be aborted. Transactional batches are marked with the transactional flag in the batch attributes. * * @return true if this batch is part of a transaction, false otherwise */ boolean isTransactional(); /** * Get the delete horizon timestamp, which indicates the time at which tombstone records and aborted * transaction markers in this batch should be removed during log compaction. * *

This value is only set for batches where tombstones or aborted transactional markers are being * cleaned up. It represents a timestamp in milliseconds. * * @return {@link OptionalLong#empty()} if the delete horizon is not set for this batch, otherwise * an OptionalLong containing the delete horizon timestamp */ OptionalLong deleteHorizonMs(); /** * Get the partition leader epoch of this record batch. This epoch is assigned by the broker when * the batch is first written and does not change with leadership transitions. * * @return The leader epoch (>= 0) or {@link #NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH} (-1) if unknown (typically * for batches produced before the leader epoch was assigned) */ int partitionLeaderEpoch(); /** * Return a streaming iterator which delays decompression of the record stream until the records are * actually requested using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the record format does not support streaming * iteration, the normal iterator is returned instead. * *

Performance characteristics: *

    *
  • For uncompressed batches: Returns a minimal iterator with no decompression overhead *
  • For compressed batches: Decompresses data on-demand as records are consumed, which can * significantly reduce memory usage for large batches *
  • Decompression buffers are obtained from the supplier, allowing buffer reuse for performance *
* *

Resource management: The returned iterator must be properly closed using * try-with-resources or explicit {@link CloseableIterator#close()} to release decompression resources. * * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression. For small * record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) * will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating. A supplier that * reuses buffers will have significant performance impact. * @return A closeable iterator over records in this batch. The iterator must be closed. * @see BufferSupplier#create() for creating a new non-reusing supplier * @see BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING for a caching-disabled supplier */ CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier); /** * Check whether this is a control batch (i.e. whether the control bit is set in the batch attributes). * Control batches contain metadata records such as end-of-transaction markers, leadership changes, etc. * For magic versions prior to 2, this always returns false. * * @return true if this batch contains control records, false otherwise */ boolean isControlBatch(); /** * Iterate all records in this batch to find the offset of the maximum timestamp. * *

Notes: *

    *
  • If multiple records have the same maximum timestamp, the earliest offset is returned *
  • Always returns {@link Optional#empty()} if the batch magic equals {@link #MAGIC_VALUE_V0} * (since V0 does not support timestamps) *
* *

Performance note: This method requires iterating through all records in the batch * and decompressing if necessary. Use with caution on large batches. * * @return {@link Optional#empty()} if no max timestamp offset exists (V0 batches), otherwise an * Optional containing the offset of the record with maximum timestamp */ default Optional offsetOfMaxTimestamp() { if (magic() == RecordBatch.MAGIC_VALUE_V0) return Optional.empty(); long maxTimestamp = maxTimestamp(); try (CloseableIterator iter = streamingIterator(BufferSupplier.create())) { while (iter.hasNext()) { Record record = iter.next(); if (maxTimestamp == record.timestamp()) return Optional.of(record.offset()); } } return Optional.empty(); } }

arguments.old_string:
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1), * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records * regardless of compression. * *

Thread-safety: RecordBatch implementations are not thread-safe. * Batches backed by ByteBuffer are particularly vulnerable to concurrent modification. Multiple threads * should not access the same batch concurrently. However, different batches can be safely accessed by * different threads in parallel. * *

Usage: Record batches are typically accessed through {@link MemoryRecords} or * {@link Records} implementations, which manage the underlying buffer lifecycle. Direct instantiation * of batch implementations is uncommon in normal usage. */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch.
     *
     * If the timestamp type is create time, this is the max timestamp among all records contained in this batch and
     * the value is updated during compaction.
     *
     * @return The max timestamp
     */
    long maxTimestamp();
arguments.new_string:
/**
     * Get the max timestamp or log append time of this record batch.
     *
     * If the timestamp type is create time ({@link TimestampType#CREATE_TIME}), this is the max
     * timestamp among all records contained in this batch and the value is updated during log compaction.
     * If the timestamp type is log append time, this value is set by the broker.
     *
     * @return The max timestamp in milliseconds
     */
    long maxTimestamp();

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
arguments.new_string:
/** * Return a streaming iterator which delays decompression of the record stream until the records are * actually requested using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the record format does not support streaming * iteration, the normal iterator is returned instead. * *

Performance characteristics: *

    *
  • For uncompressed batches: Returns a minimal iterator with no decompression overhead *
  • For compressed batches: Decompresses data on-demand as records are consumed, which can * significantly reduce memory usage for large batches *
  • Decompression buffers are obtained from the supplier, allowing buffer reuse for performance *
* *

Resource management: The returned iterator must be properly closed using * try-with-resources or explicit {@link CloseableIterator#close()} to release decompression resources. * * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression. For small * record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) * will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating. A supplier that * reuses buffers will have significant performance impact. * @return A closeable iterator over records in this batch. The iterator must be closed. * @see BufferSupplier#create() for creating a new non-reusing supplier * @see BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING for a caching-disabled supplier */ CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);

arguments.old_string:
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.
 *
 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.
 *
 * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp
 * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the
 * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp
 * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.
 *
 * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type
 * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set
 * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains
 * the previous value prior to becoming empty.
 *
 * The current attributes are given below:
 *
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 */
public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {
arguments.new_string:
/** * {@link RecordBatch} implementation for magic version 2 and above. This class encapsulates the * binary serialization format of a Kafka record batch, including the batch header and all contained * records. * *

Binary Format: The batch follows the RecordBatch schema: * 

 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
* When compression is enabled, the compressed record data follows immediately after the record count. * *

CRC Computation: The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used and covers all * bytes from the attributes field through the end of the batch. The partition leader epoch field is * excluded from CRC computation to avoid recomputation when the broker assigns a leader epoch. * *

Log Compaction: Unlike older message formats, magic v2 batches preserve the * first and last offset/sequence numbers from the original batch during log compaction. This preservation * is critical for: (1) restoring producer state on broker restart, and (2) duplicate detection via * sequence number verification. Empty batches may be retained to preserve producer sequence information * until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires. * *

Timestamp Semantics: *

    *
  • BaseTimestamp: For CREATE_TIME, reflects the first record's timestamp. After compaction, * reflects the first remaining record. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, set by the broker. If the * delete horizon flag is set, BaseTimestamp contains the horizon timestamp for cleanup.
    • *
  • MaxTimestamp: For CREATE_TIME, the maximum timestamp among current records and updated during * compaction. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, set by the broker and preserved across compaction.
    • *
* *

Batch Attributes: Encoded in a 16-bit field with the following structure: * 

 *  Bits 0-2:   Compression Type (0=None, 1=GZIP, 2=Snappy, 3=LZ4, 4=ZSTD)
 *  Bit 3:      Timestamp Type (0=CreateTime, 1=LogAppendTime)
 *  Bit 4:      Is Transactional
 *  Bit 5:      Is Control Batch
 *  Bit 6:      Delete Horizon Flag
 *  Bits 7-15:  Reserved (unused)
 *
* *

Thread-safety: {@code DefaultRecordBatch} is not thread-safe. * The underlying ByteBuffer backing a batch is mutable and can be modified by multiple threads. * To ensure thread-safety: (1) do not share batch instances across threads, or (2) use external * synchronization if sharing is necessary, or (3) wrap the batch operations with immutable views. * Multiple distinct batch instances can be safely accessed in parallel from different threads. * *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Header access (offset, timestamp, producer id, etc.) is O(1)
    • *
  • Record iteration is O(n) but amortized per-record cost is minimal for uncompressed batches
    • *
  • Decompression on iteration can be a bottleneck; use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} * with buffer reuse for memory efficiency on large batches
    • *
  • CRC validation is O(n) in batch size and is performed lazily on demand
    • *
* * @see RecordBatch * @see MemoryRecords * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder */ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

/** * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used as the reference for calculating * relative record timestamps (timestamp deltas). The interpretation of this value depends * on the timestamp type and whether the delete horizon flag is set. * *

Semantics: *

    *
  • If delete horizon is set: Contains the delete horizon timestamp for cleanup
    • *
  • If CREATE_TIME: Timestamp of the first record in the batch (or first after compaction)
    • *
  • If LOG_APPEND_TIME: Set by broker when batch is appended to log
    • *
  • If batch is empty: Set to {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP}
    • *
* * @return The base timestamp in milliseconds */ public long baseTimestamp() { return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET); }

/** * Returns a streaming iterator for records in this batch with deferred decompression. * For uncompressed batches, provides minimal overhead. For compressed batches, decompresses * data on-demand as records are consumed, reducing memory footprint compared to full upfront * decompression. * *

Resource management: The returned iterator MUST be closed using * try-with-resources or explicit close() to properly release decompression resources. * *

Performance notes: *

    *
  • For large compressed batches, this method provides significant memory savings by avoiding * full decompression until records are actually read
    • *
  • The decompression buffer supplier significantly impacts performance; reusing buffers * across multiple streaming iterators can be more efficient than allocating fresh buffers
    • *
* * @param bufferSupplier Supplies decompression buffers. For optimal performance with multiple * iterations, provide a reusing supplier via {@link BufferSupplier} methods. * @return A closeable iterator that must be closed. Never null. * @throws KafkaException if decompression fails */ @Override public CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { if (isCompressed()) return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false); else return uncompressedIterator(); }

/** * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer for reading and in-place modification * of existing record batch data. This class provides memory-efficient access to serialized Kafka * record batches without requiring disk I/O. * *

Purpose: MemoryRecords is used for three primary scenarios: *

    *
  • Reading: Deserializing record batches from a ByteBuffer (e.g., from fetch responses)
    • *
  • In-place modification: Updating batch metadata (timestamps, offsets) without copying
    • *
  • Filtering: Removing records during log compaction via {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)}
    • *
* *

Creating instances: For reading, use {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)}. * To create new batches, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} or one of the static factory methods * like {@link #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...)}. * *

Thread-safety: {@code MemoryRecords} is not thread-safe for * mutation operations like filtering or in-place batch updates. The underlying ByteBuffer is mutable * and modifications via batch methods (e.g., {@link DefaultRecordBatch#setLastOffset(long)}) directly * modify the shared buffer. Multiple threads must not concurrently: *

    *
  • Call {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)}
    • *
  • Modify batches obtained via {@link #batches()}
    • *
  • Modify or read the underlying buffer while another thread is modifying it
    • *
* * Reading batches and iterating records is thread-safe if the underlying buffer is not * concurrently modified. Different instances of MemoryRecords can be safely accessed in parallel. * *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Batch iteration: Lazy; batches are deserialized on first iteration
    • *
  • Record iteration: Depends on compression; uncompressed is O(1) per record, * compressed requires decompression
    • *
  • Filtering: O(n) where n is the number of records; decompresses if necessary
    • *
  • In-place updates: O(1) for offset/timestamp updates on DefaultRecordBatch, * but triggers CRC recomputation which is O(batch size)
    • *
  • Memory overhead: Minimal; uses provided ByteBuffer directly without copying
    • *
* * @see Records * @see RecordBatch * @see DefaultRecordBatch * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder */ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
arguments.new_string:
/** * Filters records from this MemoryRecords into a destination ByteBuffer based on a filter function. * This method is used during log compaction to remove expired tombstones and aborted transaction markers. * *

Filter Logic: For each batch: *

    *
  1. {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} is called to determine if the batch * should be completely deleted, retained empty, or checked record-by-record
    2. *
  2. For retained batches, {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)} is called * for each record to determine inclusion
    3. *
  3. Filtered batches are written with updated metadata (timestamps, offsets, delete horizon)
    4. *
* *

Performance characteristics: *

    *
  • O(n) where n is the total number of records
    • *
  • Compressed batches are decompressed using the supplied BufferSupplier; buffer reuse * significantly improves performance on large compressed batches
    • *
  • Memory: Output buffer may be reallocated if filtered data exceeds destination buffer capacity
    • *
* *

Memory management: The supplied decompressionBufferSupplier is used for * temporary buffers during decompression. For optimal performance: *

    *
  • Reuse buffers across multiple filter calls using a caching supplier
    • *
  • For small batches, allocating large buffers (64KB+) may dominate decompression cost
    • *
* * @param filter The filter determining which records to retain * @param destinationBuffer The target ByteBuffer for filtered records. May be reallocated * if insufficient space. The buffer's content after filtering * contains the output. * @param decompressionBufferSupplier Supplier for decompression buffers. For memory efficiency, * provide a reusing supplier via BufferSupplier methods. * @return A {@link FilterResult} containing: the output buffer (possibly different from input), * record/byte counts for metrics, and the maximum offset/timestamp of retained records * @see RecordFilter * @see FilterResult */ public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) { return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier); }
